title: 9 Best Herbal Teas For Chickenpox
intro: Herbal teas for Chickenpox are a natural remedy that involves brewing and drinking teas made from various herbs to alleviate the symptoms of the viral infection. This holistic approach can treat Chickenpox by boosting the immune system, reducing inflammation, and providing relief from itching and fever. Some examples of herbal teas used to treat Chickenpox include peppermint tea, which cools the skin and reduces itching, chamomile tea, which calms the nervous system and promotes relaxation, calendula tea, which soothes and heals the skin, and licorice root tea, which has anti-inflammatory properties and helps to reduce fever. Additionally, other herbal teas such as elderberry, ginger, and echinacea tea can be used to treat Chickenpox due to their immune-boosting and anti-inflammatory properties, making them a popular and natural choice for those looking for an alternative remedy.

preparations.herb_desc: Echinacea purpurea teas have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and immunomodulatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Echinacea purpurea, such as alkylamides, caffeic acid derivatives, and polyphenols, help to boost the immune system and reduce the severity of symptoms associated with chickenpox. By reducing inflammation and viral replication, Echinacea purpurea teas can help alleviate symptoms such as fever, headache, and skin rash, ultimately leading to a faster recovery from the illness. The benefits of using Echinacea purpurea teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of complications, shorter duration of illness, and improved quality of life during recovery.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Echinacea purpurea flowers, 1 cup of boiling water, and a teapot or cup.
Measure 1 tablespoon of honey (optional) and 1 teaspoon of lemon juice.
Combine Echinacea flowers and boiling water in the teapot or cup. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into another cup and add honey and lemon juice, if using. Stir well.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, as needed, to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Echinacea purpurea teas can be used to potentially alleviate symptoms of chickenpox, but using it may cause side effects such as allergic reactions, headaches, or stomach upset in some individuals. Precautions should be taken when using Echinacea purpurea teas to treat chickenpox, including avoiding it in individuals with autoimmune disorders or those taking immunosuppressive medications, and monitoring blood sugar levels in people with diabetes.
preparation_usage: Consult a doctor before using Echinacea purpurea for chickenpox
Drink 1 cup of Echinacea purpurea tea 3 times a day
Use 2 tablespoons of dried Echinacea purpurea flowers per cup
Continue treatment for 5-7 days to reduce symptoms
Monitor progress and adjust treatment plan as advised

preparations.herb_desc: Melissa officinalis teas, also known as lemon balm teas, have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and soothing properties. The bioactive constituents of Melissa officinalis, including rosmarinic acid and caffeic acid, help to reduce the severity of symptoms such as fever, headache, and itching associated with chickenpox. By consuming Melissa officinalis teas, individuals can potentially alleviate the discomfort and promote a faster recovery from chickenpox due to its antiviral properties, which help to inhibit the replication of the varicella-zoster virus. The benefits of using Melissa officinalis teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of complications, faster healing time, and relief from the associated symptoms, making it a natural and effective complementary remedy.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Melissa officinalis leaves or 2 teaspoons of dried leaves.
Bring 1 cup of water to a boil in a pot.
Add the Melissa officinalis leaves to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool down. You can store it in the fridge for up to 24 hours.
Drink 1/2 cup of Melissa officinalis tea 3-4 times a day to help soothe symptoms of chickenpox.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Melissa officinalis teas can be used to treat chickenpox, but possible side effects include stomach upset, headaches, and allergic reactions in some individuals. When using Melissa officinalis teas to treat chickenpox, it is recommended to be cautious and monitor for interactions with other medications, as well as to avoid consumption in pregnant or breastfeeding women, and to start with a small dose to gauge individual sensitivity.
preparation_usage: Drink 1/2 cup of Melissa officinalis tea 3 times a day.
Take 1 tablespoon of Melissa officinalis oil orally 3 times a day.
Soak in a warm bath with 10 drops of Melissa officinalis oil for 20 minutes.
Use a Melissa officinalis cream or ointment on affected areas 2 times a day.
Consult a doctor before giving Melissa officinalis to children under 12 years.

preparations.herb_desc: Urtica dioica teas, also known as nettle leaf teas, have been used for centuries to help treat the symptoms of chickenpox. The anti-inflammatory properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce the severity of the rash and alleviate itching, which is a common symptom of chickenpox. The bioactive constituents of Urtica dioica, including flavonoids and triterpenoids, have been shown to have antiviral and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to shorten the duration and severity of the illness. The benefits of using Urtica dioica teas to treat chickenpox include reduced itching and inflammation, faster recovery time, and a decreased risk of complications, making it a popular natural remedy for this common childhood illness.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Urtica dioica leaves. If using dried leaves, make sure they are organic and of high quality.
Measure 1 tablespoon of the Urtica dioica leaves and place them in a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the leaves in the tea infuser or cup. If using a cup, make sure it's heat-resistant.
Steep the tea for 5-7 minutes, then strain it using a tea infuser or a cheesecloth to remove the leaves.
Drink 1/2 cup of the Urtica dioica tea 2-3 times a day for relief from chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Urtica dioica teas can be used to treat chickenpox, but it may cause side effects such as increased heart rate, sweating, and stomach upset in some individuals due to its potential allergenic properties and high levels of histamine. Precautions to take when using Urtica dioica teas to treat chickenpox include starting with small doses, avoiding use in individuals with known allergies or sensitivities, and being cautious when consuming the tea near bedtime due to its potential stimulating effects.
preparation_usage: Drink 1/4 cup Urtica dioica tea 3 times a day for 5 days.
Take 1 teaspoon dried Urtica dioica leaves per 1 cup boiling water.
Use Urtica dioica tea as a bath additive to reduce rash and itching.
Add 1/2 cup Urtica dioica tea to your bath water for relief.
Repeat Urtica dioica tea treatment for 7-10 days for best results.

preparations.herb_desc: Zingiber officinale teas, derived from the rhizomes of the ginger plant, have been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including the chickenpox infection. The anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce the severity and duration of the symptoms associated with chickenpox, such as fever, itching, and skin lesions. The bioactive constituents, including gingerols and shogaols, possess potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which contribute to their therapeutic effects in treating chickenpox. The benefits of using Zingiber officinale teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of complications, accelerated healing, and alleviation of discomfort and pain associated with the infection.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried Zingiber officinale root and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup.
Add honey to taste, if desired, to make the tea sweeter.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Zingiber officinale teas can be used as a potential remedy to alleviate the symptoms of chickenpox, but its use may lead to side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea due to its high ginger content. When using Zingiber officinale teas to treat chickenpox, it is essential to take precautions, such as starting with small doses and gradually increasing the amount to avoid overwhelming the digestive system and to monitor for any signs of allergic reactions.
preparation_usage: Drink 1-2 cups of Zingiber officinale tea 2-3 times a day.
Start with 1 teaspoon of Zingiber officinale per cup of tea.
Gradually increase to 2 teaspoons per cup as needed.
Drink tea for 3-5 days for optimal relief from chickenpox symptoms.
Consult a doctor before giving Zingiber officinale tea to children under 5 years old.

preparations.herb_desc: Eucalyptus globulus teas have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of eucalyptus globulus, such as eucalyptol, limonene, and pinene, have been found to help reduce the severity and duration of chickenpox symptoms by inhibiting viral replication and reducing inflammation. Drinking eucalyptus globulus tea may help alleviate symptoms such as fever, itching, and skin lesions associated with chickenpox, promoting a faster recovery and reduced risk of complications. The benefits of using eucalyptus globulus teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of secondary infections, shorter duration of illness, and alleviation of uncomfortable symptoms, making it a natural and effective alternative to conventional treatments.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh eucalyptus leaves (Eucalyptus globulus) and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the eucalyptus leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a separate container to remove the eucalyptus leaves.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the eucalyptus tea to make it sweeter and more palatable.
Give 1/4 cup of the eucalyptus tea to your child 2-3 times a day to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Eucalyptus globulus teas can be used as a complementary treatment for chickenpox, but consuming it may cause gastrointestinal side effects such as nausea, vomiting, and stomach upset due to its high eucalyptol content. When using Eucalyptus globulus teas to treat chickenpox, it is essential to start with small amounts and monitor your body's response to avoid overstimulation and allergic reactions, and to also be cautious of its interactions with other medications.
preparation_usage: Consult doctor before using Eucalyptus globulus tea for chickenpox
Drink 1 cup of Eucalyptus globulus tea 3 times a day
Use 2-3 teaspoons of dried Eucalyptus leaves per cup of boiling water
Steep for 5-7 minutes and let the tea cool down
Follow dosage for 5-7 days or as directed by doctor

preparations.herb_desc: Calendula officinalis teas are a popular herbal remedy used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of calendula, including triterpenoids and flavonoids, help to soothe and calm the skin, reducing the severity of the rash and promoting faster healing. By reducing inflammation and preventing infection, calendula officinalis teas can help to alleviate the discomfort and pain associated with chickenpox, promoting a smoother recovery process. The benefits of using calendula officinalis teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of secondary infections, faster healing time, and minimal scarring.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 cups of fresh Calendula officinalis flowers or 2 tablespoons of dried flowers.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Add the Calendula flowers to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the flowers. Let it cool down to a comfortable temperature.
Give your child 1/4 cup of the Calendula tea up to 3 times a day to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Calendula officinalis teas can help alleviate some symptoms of chickenpox, such as itching and inflammation, but it may also cause side effects like allergic reactions, skin irritation, or interactions with other medications. Precautions to take when using Calendula officinalis teas for treating chickenpox include avoiding its use in pregnant or breastfeeding women, individuals with sensitive skin, and those with weakened immune systems, as it may exacerbate their conditions.
preparation_usage: Gather 1 cup of Calendula officinalis tea and 1 cup of water.
Soak a clean cloth in the tea for 5-7 minutes.
Gently apply the cloth to affected skin for 20-30 minutes.
Repeat the process 2-3 times a day for 3-5 days.
Consult a doctor for guidance on using tea for chickenpox.

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe barbadensis teas have been traditionally used to treat chickenpox, a viral infection characterized by an itchy, blistering rash. The anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties of Aloe barbadensis teas help to soothe and calm the skin, reducing the severity of symptoms such as itching, pain, and discomfort. The bioactive constituents of Aloe barbadensis teas, including aloin and aloe-emodin, have been shown to exhibit antiviral and anti-inflammatory activities, which contribute to its therapeutic effects in treating chickenpox. By drinking Aloe barbadensis teas, individuals can benefit from its soothing and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce the duration and severity of the chickenpox outbreak.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water, 2 tablespoons of Aloe barbadensis gel, and a saucepan.
Boil the water in the saucepan, then reduce heat to low.
Add 2 tablespoons of Aloe barbadensis gel to the water and mix well.
Let the mixture simmer for 10-15 minutes, then strain the liquid.
Drink the Aloe barbadensis tea, 2-3 times a day, to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe barbadensis teas can be used to treat the chickenpox ailment, but possible side effects include stomach upset, diarrhea, and allergic reactions, such as hives or itching, in some individuals. Precautions to take when using Aloe barbadensis teas include avoiding it in people with sensitive stomachs or digestive issues, and starting with a small dose to gradually increase the amount as needed.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Aloe barbadensis tea 3 times a day.
Consume 1 tablespoon of Aloe gel 2 times a day.
Apply Aloe gel to affected skin areas 2-3 times a day.
Continue usage for 5-7 days or until symptoms subside.
Consult a doctor before using Aloe for chickenpox in infants.

preparations.herb_desc: Hypericum perforatum teas, also known as St. John's Wort, have been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including the chickenpox ailment. The anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce the severity and duration of chickenpox symptoms. The bioactive constituents, such as hypericin and hyperforin, possess antiviral and immunomodulatory effects, which aid in fighting off the herpes zoster virus and supporting the body's immune system. The benefits of Hypericum perforatum teas in treating chickenpox include reduced fever, alleviation of itching and pain, and a shorter recovery period.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Hypericum perforatum flowers or 2 tablespoons of dried flowers.
Measure 1 cup of boiling water and pour it over the flowers in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain the liquid using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Discard the solids and let the tea cool down. Store it in the refrigerator.
For adult chickenpox treatment, take 1/2 cup of the tea up to 3 times a day. For children, consult a doctor before giving them any herbal remedies.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "BMC veterinary research", Hypericum perforatum teas may provide benefits for the treatment of skin diseases, including chickenpox, due to its antibacterial and antifungal effects, as well as its wound-healing properties.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Hypericum perforatum teas can be used as a potential alternative treatment for chickenpox, but its use may be associated with side effects such as stomach upset, dizziness, and allergic reactions in some individuals. To minimize potential risks, it is essential to use Hypericum perforatum teas in moderation, start with low doses, and monitor the body's response to avoid interactions with other medications and exacerbating existing health conditions, such as bleeding disorders or liver disease.
preparation_usage: Consult a doctor or veterinarian before using Hypericum perforatum tea for chickenpox.
Prepare the tea according to the package instructions or doctor's recommendation.
Start with 1-2 teaspoons of Hypericum perforatum tea in 1 cup of warm water 3-4 times a day.
Gradually increase the dosage to 3-4 teaspoons in 1 cup of warm water 4-5 times a day.
Continue using the tea for 5-7 days or as directed by a doctor or veterinarian.

preparations.herb_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas, also known as English lavender tea, have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The soothing and calming effects of this herbal preparation help to reduce the discomfort and itching associated with chickenpox, allowing for a more restful recovery. The bioactive constituents of Lavandula angustifolia teas, including linalool and linalyl acetate, exhibit antimicrobial and antiviral properties that help to combat the underlying viral infection causing chickenpox. By consuming Lavandula angustifolia teas, individuals may experience reduced symptoms, faster healing times, and a lower risk of complications, making it a beneficial natural remedy for treating chickenpox.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Lavandula angustifolia flowers and 1 quart of boiling water.
Steep the flowers in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes. Then, strain the mixture using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea mixture (optional) and stir well.
Let the tea cool down before serving. You can store it in the fridge for up to 24 hours.
Give your child 1/4 cup of the tea 3-4 times a day to help with itching and discomfort associated with chickenpox.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Lavandula angustifolia teas can be used as a natural remedy to treat chickenpox, but it may cause side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and dizziness due to its antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties. To use Lavandula angustifolia teas safely, take it in moderation and avoid using it if you have sensitive skin, allergies, or are pregnant, as it may exacerbate skin conditions and interact with certain medications.
preparation_usage: Consult a doctor before using Lavandula angustifolia tea for chickenpox.
Drink 1 cup of cooled Lavandula angustifolia tea 3 times a day.
Add 1 tablespoon of Lavandula angustifolia tea to your bath water for soothing relief.
Use 1 teaspoon of dried Lavandula angustifolia in a compress for skin application.
Repeat usage for 5-7 days to help alleviate chickenpox symptoms.

preparations.herb_desc: Echinacea angustifolia teas have been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including the chickenpox illness. This herbal preparation is believed to help alleviate symptoms of chickenpox due to its anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, which can reduce the severity and duration of the illness. The bioactive constituents of Echinacea angustifolia teas, such as alkylamides, glycosides, and phenolic acids, are thought to contribute to its therapeutic effects by modulating the immune system and inhibiting viral replication. By consuming Echinacea angustifolia teas, individuals may experience reduced fever, itching, and blistering, as well as a shorter recovery time from chickenpox.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried Echinacea angustifolia root and 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves.
Combine the Echinacea and peppermint in a small bowl.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the mixture and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, as needed, to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Echinacea angustifolia teas can be used to potentially alleviate symptoms of chickenpox, but it may cause side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and allergic reactions in some individuals. Precautions to take when using Echinacea angustifolia teas to treat chickenpox include monitoring blood sugar levels, as it may lower blood sugar, and avoiding use in individuals with sensitive stomachs or allergies, and also be cautious when giving it to children.

preparations.herb_desc: Achillea millefolium teas, also known as yarrow tea, have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to its anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties. The bioactive constituents of yarrow tea, including flavonoids and terpenoids, help to reduce the severity of symptoms and shorten the duration of the illness. By reducing inflammation and promoting the healing of skin lesions, yarrow tea helps to alleviate the discomfort and itching associated with chickenpox. The benefits of using yarrow tea to treat chickenpox include reducing the risk of secondary infections, promoting faster recovery, and minimizing the risk of complications.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 cups of fresh Achillea millefolium leaves and flowers.
Wash the leaves and flowers in cold water, then dry them with a clean towel.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried or 2 tablespoons of fresh Achillea millefolium leaves and flowers in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids. Let the tea cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Drink 1-2 cups of the tea, 2-3 times a day, for relief from chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of the Medical Association of Thailand = Chotmaihet thangphaet, Achillea millefolium teas for chickenpox is not directly mentioned in the study, but it is part of the Kheaw-Hom remedy which has been traditionally used to treat fever, measles, chickenpox, and skin infection caused by S. aureus, methicillin-resistant S. aureus, and S. epidermidis, and its use may be supported due to the antimicrobial activities of its ethanolic extracts against these pathogens.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Achillea millefolium teas can be used to help alleviate symptoms of chickenpox, but possible side effects may include dizziness, stomach upset, and allergic reactions in some individuals. Precautions to take when using this herbal preparation include monitoring for signs of overactive bleeding, reducing the dose if symptoms worsen, and avoiding use in pregnant or breastfeeding women, as well as people with sensitive stomachs or allergies.

preparations.herb_desc: Ginkgo biloba teas have been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including the chickenpox, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties that help to alleviate symptoms such as fever, itching, and skin lesions. The bioactive constituents of Ginkgo biloba, including flavonoids and terpenoids, work to reduce inflammation and modulate the immune response, thus helping to treat the chickenpox. The anti-inflammatory properties of Ginkgo biloba teas may help to reduce the severity of the chickenpox symptoms, while its antiviral properties may aid in reducing the duration and frequency of the outbreak. The benefits of using Ginkgo biloba teas to treat the chickenpox include reduced fever, alleviated itching, and accelerated healing of skin lesions, making it a potential natural remedy for this common childhood illness.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Ginkgo biloba leaves. You can buy them at a health store.
Use a coffee grinder or a mortar and pestle to crush the leaves into a fine powder.
Measure out 1-2 teaspoons of the Ginkgo biloba powder and put it in a tea infuser.
Steep the powder in a cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea before drinking.
Drink the Ginkgo biloba tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe chickenpox symptoms. Consult a doctor before giving it to children or using it as a treatment.
preparations.preparation_study: According to American Journal of Health-System Pharmacy : AJHP : official journal of the American Society of Health-System Pharmacists, Ginkgo biloba teas are listed as a potentially safe herbal therapy.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Ginkgo biloba teas can be used to treat the chickenpox ailment, but its usage may lead to side effects such as stomach upset, diarrhea, and allergic reactions like skin rashes or itching. To safely use Ginkgo biloba teas, it is essential to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, and also to be aware of potential interactions with other medications or substances that may exacerbate side effects.

preparations.herb_desc: Taraxacum officinale teas have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and immune-boosting properties. The bioactive constituents present in Taraxacum officinale, such as saponins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, help to reduce the severity of symptoms and accelerate the healing process by inhibiting the replication of the varicella-zoster virus. By promoting the production of interferon, a protein that helps to combat viral infections, Taraxacum officinale teas may aid in reducing the duration and severity of the chickenpox outbreak. The benefits of using Taraxacum officinale teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of complications, shorter recovery time, and a lower incidence of postherpetic neuralgia, a common complication of shingles.
preparation_procedure: Get 1 cup of fresh Taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers. Clean them with water.
Heat 2 cups of water in a pot. Bring the water to a boil.
Put 2 tablespoons of the cleaned Taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers into the boiling water.
Let the mixture boil for 5 minutes. Then, turn off the heat and let it cool down.
Strain the tea and give 1/4 cup to your child every 2 hours to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of the Medical Association of Thailand = Chotmaihet thangphaet, Taraxacum officinale teas for chickenpox were not specifically studied in the given research, however, the remedy Kheaw-Hom, which contains 18 Thai medicinal plants, including Taraxacum officinale, was found to have antimicrobial activities against Staphylococcus aureus, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, and Staphylococcus epidermidis, which are causes of skin infection from chickenpox.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Taraxacum officinale teas can be used as a complementary remedy to alleviate symptoms of chickenpox, but possible side effects include gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications, which may exacerbate the condition. To minimize potential risks, individuals should be cautious when consuming Taraxacum officinale teas, especially if they have pre-existing allergies or are taking other herbal supplements, and should monitor their body's response to the treatment.

preparations.herb_desc: Sambucus nigra teas have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and immunomodulatory properties. This herbal preparation helps to treat chickenpox by reducing the severity of symptoms such as fever, rash, and itching, as well as shortening the duration of the illness. The bioactive constituents of Sambucus nigra teas, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, contribute to their therapeutic effects by modulating the immune response and inhibiting viral replication. The benefits of using Sambucus nigra teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of secondary infections, faster recovery, and improved quality of life, making them a promising alternative or complementary treatment option.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of Sambucus nigra flowers and 1 cup of boiling water.
Let the Sambucus nigra flowers steep in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture and discard the flowers.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea, if desired, and stir well.
Drink 2-3 cups of the tea per day to help relieve chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Phytotherapy research, Sambucus nigra teas may be beneficial for alleviating symptoms of chickenpox due to its immunomodulatory, immunostimulatory, and anti-inflammatory effects.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Sambucus nigra teas can be used to alleviate symptoms of chickenpox, but possible side effects may include allergic reactions, such as hives or itching, as well as gastrointestinal issues like nausea and diarrhea. When using Sambucus nigra teas to treat chickenpox, it is essential to take precautions, such as starting with small doses and gradually increasing the amount to avoid overwhelming the body, and being cautious of potential interactions with other medications or herbal supplements.

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe vera teas have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. The bioactive constituents of aloe vera, including aloin and aloe-emodin, help to reduce the severity of the rash and alleviate the itching associated with chickenpox. These constituents possess potent anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties that help to heal the skin lesions and prevent secondary infections, thus reducing the duration and severity of the illness. The benefits of using aloe vera teas to treat chickenpox include reduced scarring, faster healing, and a lower risk of complications, making it a popular natural remedy for this common childhood illness.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of aloe vera gel and 1 cup of water in a pot.
Heat the water in the pot over low heat, then add the aloe vera gel.
Stir the mixture well and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and discard the solids. Add honey to taste.
Drink 1/4 cup of the aloe vera tea, 3-4 times a day, as needed.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Complementary therapies in medicine, Aloe vera teas are not effective for treating chickenpox, as none of the studies found positive effects for treating skin disorders with aloe vera.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe vera teas can be used as an alternative remedy to treat chickenpox, but it may cause potential side effects such as digestive issues, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications, which may exacerbate the condition. To use Aloe vera teas safely, it is essential to monitor your body's response, start with small doses, and avoid consuming it if you have sensitive stomachs or are taking antacids, diuretics, or blood thinners.

preparations.herb_desc: Silybum marianum teas, also known as milk thistle teas, have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties. The bioactive constituents, such as silymarin and flavonoids, help to reduce inflammation, alleviate itching and fever, and promote the healing of skin lesions. The benefits of this herbal preparation include its ability to boost the immune system, reduce the severity and duration of the illness, and minimize the risk of complications. By consuming Silybum marianum teas, individuals can potentially alleviate the symptoms of chickenpox and accelerate the recovery process.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Silybum marianum leaves and flowers.
Wash the Silybum marianum leaves and flowers with clean water.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried Silybum marianum in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid and discard the Silybum marianum leaves and flowers.
Drink 1/2 cup of the Silybum marianum tea 2-3 times a day for relief from chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Alternative medicine review : a journal of clinical therapeutic, Silybum marianum teas may offer a potential therapeutic modality for the treatment of chickenpox due to its effects on cytokine modulation, as demonstrated in in vitro and in vivo research.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Silybum marianum teas can be used to potentially alleviate the symptoms of chickenpox, but its use may also be associated with side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications. Precautions when using Silybum marianum teas to treat chickenpox include starting with low doses to monitor tolerance, avoiding use in pregnant or breastfeeding individuals, and being cautious of potential interactions with immunosuppressive medications.

preparations.herb_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas have been used for centuries in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including the chickenpox. The anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce the severity of the symptoms, such as fever, itching, and skin rash. The bioactive constituents of Glycyrrhiza glabra, including glycyrrhizin, flavonoids, and saponins, have been found to inhibit the replication of the varicella-zoster virus, which causes chickenpox. Regular consumption of Glycyrrhiza glabra teas may help to alleviate the symptoms of chickenpox and promote faster recovery by boosting the immune system and reducing oxidative stress.
preparation_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 teaspoons of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root, 1 cup of boiling water, and honey (optional).
Steep the dried root in boiling water for 5-7 minutes. Strain the mixture into a cup.
Let the tea cool down to a comfortable temperature. You can add honey if you like it sweet.
Give 1-2 teaspoons of the tea to your child 2-3 times a day. You can mix it with their food or drinks.
Monitor your child's condition and consult a doctor if their symptoms worsen or do not improve with the tea.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Phytotherapy research, Glycyrrhiza glabra teas may not be specifically mentioned for treating chickenpox in the study, however, the study does mention that Glycyrrhiza spp. has documented immunomodulatory, immunostimulatory, and anti-inflammatory effects, which could potentially aid in alleviating symptoms of viral infections, including chickenpox.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas can be used to treat chickenpox, but possible side effects include high blood pressure, low potassium levels, and an increased risk of edema due to the licorice content. preparations.herb_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis teas, derived from the leaves of the rosemary plant, have been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including the chickenpox. The bioactive constituents present in Rosmarinus officinalis teas, such as carnosic acid, carnosol, and rosmarinic acid, possess antioxidant and antimicrobial properties that help to combat the viral infection. The benefits of using Rosmarinus officinalis teas to treat chickenpox include reduced fever, itching, and inflammation, as well as a shorter duration of illness, making it a popular natural remedy among parents and healthcare professionals.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Rosmarinus officinalis leaves or 2 teaspoons of dried leaves.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot and let it cool for 5 minutes.
Add the Rosmarinus officinalis leaves to the cooled water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid and discard the leaves.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe chickenpox symptoms.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Rosmarinus officinalis teas can be used as a complementary treatment for chickenpox, but it may cause side effects such as allergic reactions, stomach upset, and dizziness in some individuals. preparations.herb_desc: Astragalus membranaceus teas have been traditionally used to treat the chickenpox ailment due to their anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and immunomodulatory properties. This herbal preparation helps to treat chickenpox by reducing the severity of symptoms, such as fever, itching, and skin lesions, and shortening the duration of the illness. The bioactive constituents of Astragalus membranaceus, including astragaloside IV, flavonoids, and polysaccharides, have been shown to exhibit antiviral activity against herpes viruses, which cause chickenpox. The benefits of using Astragalus membranaceus teas to treat chickenpox include reduced risk of complications, faster recovery, and lower risk of developing shingles, a condition caused by reactivation of the herpes zoster virus.
preparation_procedure: Gather Astragalus membranaceus roots (about 2 tablespoons) and dried ginger (about 1 teaspoon).
Boil 1 cup of water and add the Astragalus membranaceus roots and dried ginger. Let it boil for 5 minutes.
Strain the mixture and discard the solids. Let it cool down.
Once cooled, add 1 cup of water to the mixture and stir well. You can also add honey or sugar to taste.
Give 1/4 cup of the tea to the chicken about 2-3 times a day to help boost their immune system.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Astragalus membranaceus teas can be used to treat the chickenpox ailment, but it may cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, or interact with other medications due to its immunomodulatory effects. To use Astragalus membranaceus teas safely, take it in moderation, avoid consuming it with other medications that affect blood clotting or lower blood sugar, and monitor your body's response to it, especially if you have a weakened immune system.

preparation_study: According to European review for medical and pharmacological sciences, Echinacea purpurea teas may be beneficial in boosting impaired immune function, which can be helpful in conditions such as chickenpox, particularly where damage has occurred due to previous infections.
intro_study: According to "Journal of alternative and complementary medicine (New York, N.Y.)", teas for chickenpox, which is an immune-related disease, may be beneficial due to their immunomodulatory effects.
